The Service Status Summary component reacts to the latest getSystemStatusOverview response and highlights the currently focused service (if any) based on the active serviceSlug state.

The Incident List component filters the incidents received from getSystemStatusOverview to those that match the currently focused service’s id via affected_service_ids when a serviceSlug is set; when no serviceSlug is set, it shows incidents for all services.

The Incident List Filters component allows the user to refine visible incidents (e.g., show only is_current incidents or filter by StatusIncident.status or StatusIncident.impact) by updating local filter state; the Incident List applies this state without new API calls.

When the user clicks an incident row in Incident List, the page navigates to status_incident.html with incidentId set to the clicked StatusIncident.id via window location changes, preserving no additional parameters.

If sourceArticleId is present in the URL, the Navigation Context Indicator component displays contextual messaging that the user came from a specific help article and keeps sourceArticleId intact when updating serviceSlug in the URL (e.g., when changing the focused service).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page maintains its primary state via URL parameters and the latest response from getSystemStatusOverview. On load, the page reads the current URL. If a serviceSlug parameter is present and non-empty, it is treated as the active service focus. The page then calls getSystemStatusOverview(serviceSlug), which returns arrays of StatusService and StatusIncident entities. If serviceSlug is absent, the page calls getSystemStatusOverview without parameters to retrieve a full overview. The active serviceSlug is stored in in-memory state and mirrored in the URL so refreshes or direct navigation preserve the selected focus. sourceArticleId, if present, is treated as contextual information only; it is not used in API calls but is preserved when updating the serviceSlug parameter. Any time the user changes the service selection, the new serviceSlug replaces the previous value in the URL (or is removed entirely if the user clears the filter), and the page re-invokes getSystemStatusOverview with the updated parameter. Incident-level filters such as 'show only active incidents' are maintained in in-memory state only and do not affect URL parameters; they are applied client-side by filtering the incidents array returned by getSystemStatusOverview. No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client-side storage. All navigation to the Incident Detail page uses the incidentId URL parameter only, constructed from the selected StatusIncident.id.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, calls to getSystemStatusOverview, global loading/error state, and provides the services and incidents data, along with active filter state (activeServiceSlug and incident-level filters), to child components.

Uses the presence of sourceArticleId in the URL to display contextual information that the user arrived from a troubleshooting article and to ensure that sourceArticleId is preserved when serviceSlug is changed by user actions.

Displays a selectable list (or equivalent control) of services from the StatusService array and allows users to focus on a specific service or clear the focus. When the user selects or clears a service, the component triggers changes in the active serviceSlug and updates the URL via the controller.

Shows the current status for each service (e.g., operational, degraded_performance) using the StatusService array returned by getSystemStatusOverview. Highlights or marks the service that matches the current activeServiceSlug, and may present an aggregate overall status derived from all services.

Allows users to adjust local filters on the incidents dataset (for example by StatusIncident.status, StatusIncident.impact, or whether StatusIncident.is_current is true). Updates in-memory filter state in the controller without making additional API calls.

Displays the list of incidents returned by getSystemStatusOverview, filtered by the active service (if activeServiceSlug is set) and by any local incident filters. Each row includes key fields from StatusIncident and indicates which services are affected using StatusService information. Clicking a row navigates to status_incident.html with the corresponding incidentId.
Shows a loading state while getSystemStatusOverview requests are in progress and hides itself when the response is received or an error occurs.
Displays an error message when getSystemStatusOverview fails or returns an error. Offers a user action to retry loading by re-invoking the controller’s data fetch logic.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container. On desktop, main article spans roughly 8 columns and the related-articles sidebar spans 4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the support-center pattern from the mockup: a full-width dark global header with brand and a large centered search bar (outside the listed components), followed by a light-content area. Inside this area, the navigation-context-indicator (breadcrumb) sits as a single row at the very top of the content column. Directly beneath it sits the page H1 "System Status Overview" (part of the page shell, not a listed component). Below the H1, the status-overview-controller manages a two-column layout: the left, wider column houses the primary status card (service-status-summary with embedded service-filter-controls at its top), and the right, narrower column houses the secondary card containing the incident-list, with incident-list-filters in its header area. The status-error-notice appears as a full-width alert row between the H1 and the two-column region when needed. The status-loading-indicator appears as a centered overlay over the two-column content when data is loading.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the support-center pattern from the mockup: a full-width dark global header with brand and a large centered search bar (outside the listed components), followed by a light-content area. Inside this area, the navigation-context-indicator (breadcrumb) sits as a single row at the very top of the content column. Directly beneath it sits the page H1 "System Status Overview" (part of the page shell, not a listed component). Below the H1, the status-overview-controller manages a two-column layout: the left, wider column houses the primary status card (service-status-summary with embedded service-filter-controls at its top), and the right, narrower column houses the secondary card containing the incident-list, with incident-list-filters in its header area. The status-error-notice appears as a full-width alert row between the H1 and the two-column region when needed. The status-loading-indicator appears as a centered overlay over the two-column content when data is loading.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The dark header occupies roughly 15–20% of the viewport height on desktop, with a long, centered search bar. Below, the main content is placed within a centered max-width container (approximately 1040–1200px), leaving about 15–20% horizontal whitespace on each side on large screens. Within this container, the breadcrumb, H1, and any error notice span the full content width. The two-column area controlled by status-overview-controller uses a 12-column grid: the left column (current status) spans about 8 columns (~66% of content width) and the right column (recent incidents) spans 4 columns (~34%). Vertically, there is 24–32px between header and breadcrumb, 16–24px between breadcrumb and H1, and 24–32px between H1 (or error notice) and the two-column cards. Inside cards, padding is around 24–32px on all sides, with 16–24px gaps between internal sections (titles, filters, lists). The incident card is intentionally shorter than the status card, leaving comfortable whitespace below it.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderately spacious. Text blocks, filters, and lists are separated with clear but not excessive spacing, generally using 8–16px gaps between closely related elements and 16–24px between distinct sections. Within cards, list items in the status summary and incident list are single-column, vertically stacked rows that are easy to scan, with generous line height. Controls in the filter areas are compact but not cramped, aligning horizontally where possible and wrapping gracefully on smaller widths. The aim is a readable, businesslike interface that avoids both clutter and emptiness.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup. The eye first lands on the dark header and centered search bar, then moves down to the breadcrumb on the left, which orients the user within the help center. From there it drops to the large H1 “System Status Overview,” establishing page context. The primary flow then moves into the left main card, starting with the service-filter-controls at the top, then naturally down into the service-status-summary details. In parallel, the right column incident card is visible in peripheral vision as a secondary focus, drawing the eye for deeper investigation of specific incidents. The error notice, when present, intercepts attention immediately after the H1, while the loading indicator, when active, momentarily dominates the central area to signal system activity without altering the broader structural layout.

layout.overall_description: The System Status Overview page sits beneath a dark, full-width help-center header with a prominent centered search bar. Below this header, a light-background content area is centered within a wide but constrained container. At the top of this content column, a subtle breadcrumb row anchors the user’s location in the help center. Just beneath the breadcrumb, a large, confidently set H1 “System Status Overview” announces the page purpose. When errors occur, a slim but noticeable alert bar appears below the title. Beneath this top context band, the page splits into two cards aligned on a 12-column grid: a broad left card showing the current platform status and filters, and a narrower right card listing recent incidents. Both cards share the same visual language—softly rounded corners, a light surface, and generous internal padding—making them feel like related panels within a single system. The left card is taller and more dominant, containing filters and a structured list of services and their current health, forming the primary interaction space. The right card, slightly shorter, stacks incident filters above a scrollable list of recent incidents, functioning as a concise, scannable sidebar. Vertical and horizontal spacing is consistent and measured, creating a clean, corporate feel that guides the user from high-level status at left to detailed incident exploration at right, all within a familiar help-center framework.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status-overview-controller is the structural wrapper for all main content below the H1 (and any error notice) in the light-background area. It is positioned centrally on the page within the max-width content container, starting approximately 24–32px below the H1 or error bar. Horizontally, it spans the entire usable content width (about 80–70% of the viewport on large screens, with 15–20% margins on each side). Inside this wrapper, the layout is divided into two columns on desktop: an 8-column left region for the primary status card (service-status-summary and its embedded service-filter-controls) and a 4-column right region for the incident card (incident-list and incident-list-filters). Both columns share the same top alignment, forming a clean horizontal row of cards. On tablet and mobile, the controller reflows to a single-column stack where the left (status) card appears first and the right (incidents) card is placed below it, each spanning 100% of the content width with 24–32px vertical separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container that defines the grid for its children. On larger screens it enforces a fixed 8/4 split; on smaller screens it reflows to a vertical stack while preserving consistent lateral margins. It does not scroll independently; it participates in the page’s normal vertical scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-context-indicator is the breadcrumb row placed just below the global dark header. It is aligned to the left edge of the centered content container and spans nearly the full container width, sitting on a light background. Vertically, there is about 24–32px of space between the bottom edge of the dark header and the top of the breadcrumb, and about 16–24px of space between the breadcrumb and the H1 below. The breadcrumb text sits on a single line when possible, left-justified, with ample horizontal breathing room on its left and right. It is visually slim—roughly 32–40px in height—making it feel like a lightweight orientation strip that doesn’t compete with the H1 or cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, sits at the top of the content column and scrolls away with the page. It does not stick or float; its role is to provide contextual orientation before the main two-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-filter-controls sit at the very top of the left main card, just inside the top padding of the card that also houses the service-status-summary. Within that card, they form a horizontal control band directly beneath the card’s internal heading (e.g., “Current Platform Status”). The filter controls align left, occupying roughly the first 60–70% of the card width on desktop, with controls arranged in a row—such as dropdowns, pill toggles, or checkboxes—with 8–16px spacing between each. If there is a secondary action (e.g., reset filters), it aligns to the right side of the same row, keeping the band visually balanced. There is about 16px of vertical space below the card heading and 16–24px of space between the bottom of the filters and the top of the service-status-summary content, preserving a clear hierarchy: title, then filters, then status data.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left card; it scrolls with the card content and does not float independently. On narrower viewports, the controls wrap onto multiple lines but remain grouped tightly near the top of the card, preserving a compact, easily scannable filter area above the status summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-status-summary occupies the bulk of the left main card beneath the service-filter-controls. It is contained within the same card surface, using the remaining vertical space and about 90–95% of the card’s inner width (respecting 24–32px side padding). The content is arranged as a vertical list or grid of services and their statuses, aligned to the left and extending downward in a single column or two-column subgrid, depending on design. Each service row uses a consistent layout: service name on the left, status indicator (text and/or color-coded badge) on the right, with 8–16px vertical spacing between rows. The top of the summary sits roughly 16–24px below the filters; the bottom of the card leaves 24–32px padding before the card’s edge, so the last row never feels cramped. This left card is the taller of the two, typically extending below the bottom of the right incident card and occupying a substantial portion of the visible viewport height, reinforcing its primacy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column of the status-overview-controller. It grows vertically with content. On smaller screens, it expands to full width and appears immediately beneath the filter controls, which are still within the same card block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The incident-list-filters are placed at the top of the right-hand card that also contains the incident-list. Inside this card, a concise title such as “Recent Incidents” appears at the top-left, and the filters sit directly below or aligned on the same row, depending on available width. On desktop, the preferred layout is a 1-row, 2-zone structure inside the card: the card title at the far left, and a small cluster of filters (e.g., date range, severity) right-aligned on the same horizontal line, with 16–24px separation between title and filters. If space is limited, the filters break onto a second line beneath the title, left-aligned, but still within the top 64–80px of the card. There is consistent 16–24px spacing between the bottom of the filter area and the first row of the incident-list items below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right card, scrolling away as the user scrolls the page. It does not stick to the top of the viewport. On narrow viewports, the entire right card—including these filters—stacks below the left card and spans full width, with the filters remaining at the top of this card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The incident-list fills the main body of the right-hand card, directly beneath the incident-list-filters. It occupies the full inner width of the card minus standard side padding (24–32px), presenting incidents as a vertically stacked list of rows. Each incident row spans the card width, with key information (incident title, status, date/time, affected services) arranged left to right in a clean, single-line or two-line layout, separated by subtle dividers or consistent vertical spacing of 8–12px. The list typically reaches mid to lower page height but is shorter than the left status card, leaving intentional whitespace beneath the card in the right column. This whitespace helps emphasize that incidents are secondary information compared to the primary service-status-summary. If the list grows long, the page scrolls normally; the card does not use an internal scroll region, favoring a single continuous page scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column. It extends vertically as needed with the page scroll. On smaller devices, the incident-list appears below the left status card, maintaining the same card styling and full-width presentation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status-loading-indicator appears when status or incident data is being fetched. In a global loading state, it is centrally positioned over the two-column content region controlled by the status-overview-controller. Visually, it sits in the middle of the combined area where the left and right cards would normally appear—approximately centered horizontally within the content container and vertically in the space between the H1 and the fold of the viewport. It may be accompanied by a subtle dimming of the card areas or temporary placeholders, but the core behavior is that the spinner or progress element is clearly visible at the center of the main content area without shifting the breadcrumb or H1. When loading only a subset (e.g., incident data), a smaller inline version can appear centered within the affected card’s body area, replacing its list content while preserving the card’s overall position and size.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces main card content without changing the structural layout. In full loading states, it behaves like an overlay centered within the status-overview-controller’s bounds, not fixed to the viewport edges, and scrolls out of view if the user scrolls aggressively. In partial loading states, it is inline within the specific card that is updating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status-error-notice is displayed as a full-width alert bar within the content column when data cannot be loaded or a significant error occurs. It appears directly beneath the H1 “System Status Overview” and above the two-column area managed by status-overview-controller, spanning the same width as the cards. Vertically, it uses a compact but noticeable height—around 48–64px—with 16–24px of space above and below to separate it from the H1 and the cards. The message text is left-aligned with an icon on the far left if used, and the bar uses a subtle but contrasting background color to stand out against the standard light background, while still fitting the minimalist, corporate aesthetic. When present, the cards below remain in their usual position, simply pushed down by the height of the notice so the overall structural relationships (breadcrumb → H1 → alert → two-column cards) are preserved.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline within the main vertical flow of the page. It does not float or overlay content; it occupies its own row and scrolls with the page. It appears and disappears based on error state without affecting the layout of the header or breadcrumb.

name: Incident Detail & Report Issue
title: Incident Detail & Report Issue
description: Displays detailed information for a specific system status incident and lets users submit incident reports linked to that incident.

core_features: Load and display incident details for the incidentId provided in the URL using getStatusIncidentDetail.
Show affected platform services for the selected incident using data from getStatusIncidentDetail.
Display key timestamps for the incident (such as start, last update, and optional resolution) as a simple timeline derived from the StatusIncident fields.
Provide a prominent control to open or scroll to an incident report form associated with the currently viewed incident.
Load incident report form configuration (allowed severities and default severity) using getIncidentReportFormConfig.
Render an incident report form with subject, severity selection, and description fields, bound to the current incident.
On form submission, call submitIncidentReport so the report is logged against the current incident.
Provide user feedback after submission (e.g., confirmation state) based on the IncidentReport returned from submitIncidentReport.
Allow navigation back to the System Status Overview page while preserving no local state (navigation purely via URL).
user_workflows:
View incident details:
1. User navigates to status_incident.html with an incidentId parameter in the URL.
2. On page load, the page reads incidentId from the URL.
3. The page calls getStatusIncidentDetail with incidentId.
4. When data is returned, the incident detail components display the incident title, status, impact, affected services, and key timestamps.
5. User can review the information, including the services impacted and incident status, without interacting with the form.

Open the incident report form from the incident detail:
1. User is viewing incident details for a specific incidentId.
2. User clicks the primary "Report an issue" control located in the incident detail area.
3. The page updates its internal view state (e.g., expands the report form section or scrolls to it).
4. If the report form configuration has not yet been loaded, the page calls getIncidentReportFormConfig with the same incidentId.
5. Once configuration is loaded, the report form renders with severity options and any default severity applied.

Fill and submit an incident report:
1. User locates the incident report form associated with the currently viewed incident.
2. User types a short subject describing their issue into the subject input field.
3. User selects a severity from the severity control, which is populated from allowed_severities returned by getIncidentReportFormConfig. The control initializes to default_severity if provided.
4. User enters additional details into the description text area, such as error codes or observed behavior related to the ongoing incident.
5. User clicks the "Submit report" button to send the report.
6. The form validates that required fields (including subject and severity) are populated.
7. On successful validation, the page calls submitIncidentReport with incidentId, subject, severity, and description.
8. When submitIncidentReport returns an IncidentReport, the page displays a confirmation state referencing the submitted report (e.g., showing its subject or timestamp) and may optionally clear or disable the form to prevent accidental duplicate submissions.

Navigate back to system status overview:
1. While viewing incident details or after submitting a report, the user clicks a breadcrumb or a "Back to system status" control.
2. The page navigates to status_overview.html via a link; no local state is stored.
3. Any future return to the incident detail page requires incidentId in the URL again to reload the data.
interactions: On page load, the page reads incidentId from the URL and triggers data loading via getStatusIncidentDetail. If incidentId is missing or invalid, an error state component is shown and no incident details or form are rendered.
Clicking the breadcrumb or a dedicated "Back to system status" link navigates to status_overview.html using a standard anchor link without additional parameters (unless defined by that target page).
Clicking the "Report an issue" control toggles or reveals the incident report form section and, if necessary, initiates a call to getIncidentReportFormConfig with the current incidentId.

Changing the severity dropdown in the form updates the local form state but does not alter any backend data until form submission. The available options are populated exclusively from allowed_severities returned by getIncidentReportFormConfig.
Typing into the subject input updates the form's subject value, which must pass basic non-empty validation before submission.
Typing into the description textarea updates the form's description value; this field is optional or required based on validation rules defined on the page (not the API).
Clicking the "Submit report" button triggers client-side validation. If validation fails, inline validation messages appear and submitIncidentReport is not called.

On successful validation, clicking "Submit report" calls submitIncidentReport with incidentId derived from the URL and the current form field values. During the request, the submit button is disabled or shows a loading state to prevent multiple submissions.

When submitIncidentReport successfully returns, the page updates to a confirmation state (e.g., success message component bound to the returned IncidentReport). Optionally, the form can be cleared or collapsed after a successful submission.
If submitIncidentReport returns an error or fails, an error feedback component displays a problem message and re-enables the form so the user can retry.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary source of page state is the incidentId URL parameter. On every page load or URL change, the page reads incidentId from the query string of status_incident.html. If incidentId is present, getStatusIncidentDetail is called with that value to load the incident and its affected services. All incident detail components rely on this data and do not render content until it is available. The incidentId from the URL is also used to request form configuration via getIncidentReportFormConfig when the incident report form is first shown. No incident data or configuration is kept in persistent storage; state is refreshed whenever the URL (and thus incidentId) changes. Optional view state, such as whether the report form is expanded or collapsed, can be encoded in additional URL parameters (for example, a boolean-like parameter that indicates the initial open/closed state). If such a parameter is present and indicates that the form should be shown, the page will automatically reveal the form and call getIncidentReportFormConfig once incident details are loaded. On form submission, incidentId is always taken from the current URL rather than any cached value to ensure consistency. All navigation away from this page (e.g., back to status_overview.html) is handled by links that set the destination URL explicitly, without relying on localStorage or other persistent client-side mechanisms.

components.functionality: Parses and exposes URL parameters relevant to this page, primarily incidentId and any optional view-state flags (such as whether to auto-open the report form). Validates the presence of incidentId and provides it to other components.

Uses the incidentId provided by the URL state manager to call getStatusIncidentDetail. Manages loading and error states for incident data and passes the loaded StatusIncident and StatusService array to display components.
Displays error messages when incidentId is missing or when getStatusIncidentDetail fails. Provides an optional link or action to navigate back to the status overview page.
Shows a simple breadcrumb with a link back to the System Status Overview and the current incident title once loaded.

Displays high-level incident information including title, current status, impact, and a prominent "Report an issue" action. The status and impact values are taken directly from the StatusIncident object returned by getStatusIncidentDetail.
Lists all affected services for the incident using the affected_services array returned by getStatusIncidentDetail. Each entry shows the service name and status from the StatusService model.

Shows a simple chronological view of the incident using timestamps from the StatusIncident object, such as started_at, updated_at, and resolved_at (if present). It does not create additional timeline entries beyond what can be derived from these fields.
Displays the incident description from the StatusIncident data, if provided, giving users more context about the issue.

On demand, uses the current incidentId to call getIncidentReportFormConfig. Exposes incident_summary, allowed_severities, and default_severity to the incident report form and manages loading and error states specific to the form configuration.

Renders and manages the incident report form bound to the currently viewed incident. Includes fields for subject (text input), severity (selection control populated from allowed_severities with default_severity preselected), and description (multiline text input). Handles validation, submission, and local form state changes.

Coordinates the actual submitIncidentReport call. Collects incidentId from URL state and field values from the incident-report-form component, validates them, invokes submitIncidentReport, and passes the resulting IncidentReport to confirmation or error feedback components.

Displays a success message and key details from the IncidentReport returned by submitIncidentReport, such as the report subject and created_at timestamp. Can also offer secondary actions like resetting the form or leaving the page.
Displays a clear error state if submitIncidentReport fails or if getIncidentReportFormConfig returns an error. Offers retry actions that re-trigger the relevant API call.

Provides a direct navigation link from the incident detail page back to status_overview.html, independent of other components. Can be used within multiple sections such as the header, error states, or confirmation panels.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container. On desktop, main article spans roughly 8 columns and the related-articles sidebar spans 4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Follow the support-center pattern from the mockup: a dark, full-width global header with brand and search at the very top (site chrome, not part of these components). Below that, the main content sits in a centered, max-width container. Inside this container, show a breadcrumb row first, then a page-level incident summary header as the full-width H1-equivalent. Beneath the summary, the layout splits into two columns: a wider left column for the incident details (description, affected services, timeline) arranged as a continuous, card-style article; and a narrower right column for a single, compact card containing the report-incident form and its related feedback states. Error banners and confirmation messages appear tightly associated with the content they affect (either at the top of the main content area or within the right-hand form card).

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Follow the support-center pattern from the mockup: a dark, full-width global header with brand and search at the very top (site chrome, not part of these components). Below that, the main content sits in a centered, max-width container. Inside this container, show a breadcrumb row first, then a page-level incident summary header as the full-width H1-equivalent. Beneath the summary, the layout splits into two columns: a wider left column for the incident details (description, affected services, timeline) arranged as a continuous, card-style article; and a narrower right column for a single, compact card containing the report-incident form and its related feedback states. Error banners and confirmation messages appear tightly associated with the content they affect (either at the top of the main content area or within the right-hand form card).
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use a centered content column with generous side gutters (approximately 15–20% viewport width on each side on large screens, with a max content width around 1040–1200px). The dark global header occupies the top 15–20% of the viewport height. Within the main container, the breadcrumb, summary header, and action/link rows are full-width. Below the summary, the page splits roughly into a 2/3 width left column and 1/3 width right column. Cards in both columns use 24–32px internal padding and 24–32px vertical gaps between major blocks. Vertical spacing from header → breadcrumb → summary → cards follows 24–40px increments derived from the 8px base grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious density: comfortable but not sparse. Line heights are generous, with 4–8px between inline items (chips, badges), 12–16px between closely related text lines, and 24px+ between subsections. Inside cards, body text and lists are compact and easy to scan, but with enough breathing room so each section (description, services, timeline, form fields) is clearly delineated. Lists are single-column, vertically stacked, with consistent spacing to support scanning at a glance.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Start with the high-contrast dark header and central search (site chrome), then guide the eye down to the breadcrumb on the left, then to the prominent incident summary header (H1) just below it. From this anchor, the eye moves into the main two-column region: the left incident details card is the primary reading path and dominates attention, while the right report form card is a prominent secondary focus. Within the left card, the flow is top-down: description, then affected services, then the timeline. Within the right card, the flow is also top-down: optional error banner, form content, then confirmation panel if shown. Any top-level error banner for loading the incident appears immediately below the header, before the breadcrumb, interrupting the flow with strong color so it is noticed first when present.

layout.overall_description: The page sits beneath a dark, full-width help-center header with a centered search bar, giving a familiar support-center feel. Below this, a light-background content area is centered in the viewport, with wide left and right margins. At the top of this content area, a slim breadcrumb row stretches across the full width, followed by a compact "Back to status" text link aligned to the left, and then a bold incident summary header acting as the page’s H1. Under this header, the layout splits into two columns: on the left, a large, single card-like block spans about two-thirds of the width, containing the incident’s descriptive text, the list of affected services, and the chronological timeline stacked vertically with clear dividers and consistent spacing. On the right, a shorter card occupies the remaining third of the width, aligned to the top of the left card, housing the "Report this issue" form, its inline error banner, and its confirmation state. The left card is taller and drives the primary reading experience; the right card is shorter, leaving comfortable white space below. Overall, the structure mirrors a knowledge-base article with a related sidebar: clean, minimal, and grid-aligned, with the user’s eye moving naturally from global context (header, breadcrumb) into the incident details on the left and then to the reporting action on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual state manager that lives logically at the top of the page’s component tree, just inside the main content container. It does not render any UI of its own and therefore occupies no visible space; it simply determines which incident to load based on the URL and orchestrates what other components show. All visible layout is unaffected by its physical position and only responds to the state it provides.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no rendered DOM footprint. Conceptually sits above all incident-detail components as an invisible controller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is also non-visual. It resides near the top of the incident detail area, tightly coupled with the incident URL state. While loading, it may indirectly trigger a lightweight inline loading state within the incident summary header or main details card, but it does not itself occupy a distinct visible region. Its functional placement precedes the rendering of the breadcrumb, summary header, and detail sections, but visually it leaves the spatial layout entirely to those components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no dedicated space. Controls when downstream visual components render their content or loading states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When present, this feedback banner appears as a full-width bar at the very top of the main content container, directly beneath the dark global header and above the breadcrumb row. It spans the same centered max-width as the rest of the page content, with 16–24px internal vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding, aligned left. The banner uses high-contrast coloring (e.g., red accent on a light background) and sits on its own row with 16–24px vertical margin to the breadcrumb below, clearly separating it from other content. It pushes all other content downward, preserving the linear, top-to-bottom flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element; scrolls with the page. Only renders when there is an error; otherwise no space is reserved.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb is displayed as a single, thin row directly below the error banner (if present) or the global header if not. It is aligned to the left within the centered main content container, occupying the full available width but with its text constrained to the left half or so for readability. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical space above the breadcrumb (to the header or banner) and 8–16px below it, leading into the subsequent navigation link and summary header. The breadcrumb sits on the light background, with subtle typography that clearly indicates hierarchy without overpowering the main title beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline-block row within the normal document flow; scrolls with the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The "Back to status" navigation link appears immediately below the breadcrumb and above the incident summary header, aligned to the left edge of the content column. It occupies a single line of text with a small, subtle arrow icon to its left, and sits on its own row with about 8–12px of vertical padding above and below, giving it enough separation to be noticeable without competing visually with the H1. The link does not span the full width; instead it hugs the left side, leaving the remainder of the row as open whitespace, reinforcing its role as a lightweight navigational aid rather than a major structural element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text link in the normal flow; scrolls with content. No sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The incident summary header functions as the page’s H1 block and sits centered within the main content container, directly beneath the back-to-status link. It spans the full width of the content column but is visually left-aligned. The incident title, status badge, and key metadata (e.g., current state, start time) are arranged horizontally and vertically within this block with 16–24px internal spacing, following a clean, compact pattern. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space above it (from the back link) and 24–32px below it before the two-column layout begins. It does not sit inside a card; instead, it appears as clean text and badges directly on the page background, providing a strong visual anchor for both columns underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static header block; scrolls with the page. Establishes the top edge from which the two-column layout below is aligned.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The description block appears at the top of the left-hand column within a large, primary card dedicated to incident details. This card begins approximately 24–32px below the incident summary header. The card itself extends the full width of the left column (roughly 66% of the content width), with 24–32px internal padding on all sides and a subtle border or shadow. The description block occupies the upper portion of this card, starting at the top-left with a section heading (e.g., "Incident overview") followed by descriptive body text. The text flows in a single column with comfortable line spacing and 12–16px between paragraphs. There is about 24px of vertical space between the end of the description text and the start of the next section (affected-services list) within the same card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section inside the left-column card. It expands vertically with content but does not affect the card’s left/right boundaries.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The affected-services list is the middle section within the same left-column incident details card, directly beneath the description block. It begins with a small subheading aligned left, followed by a vertical list of affected services. Each service appears as a single row or short line, with 8–12px of vertical spacing between items. The list respects the card’s internal padding and does not exceed the column width. There is a subtle visual separator (e.g., a thin horizontal rule or increased spacing of 16–24px) between the description block above and this list, and another 24px of vertical space before the timeline section below. This keeps the entire left card feeling like one structured article while clearly segmenting each part.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list section within the left-column card; grows in height with the number of services listed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeline panel forms the bottom section of the main incident details card in the left column. It is aligned to the left within the same card container and spans the card’s full inner width. A section heading (e.g., "Timeline") appears first, followed by a vertically stacked timeline of events, each event occupying a row with timestamp and description aligned in a structured way. Events are separated by 12–16px of vertical spacing and possibly a subtle vertical timeline rule on the left. There is about 24px of top padding before the first timeline item and 24–32px of bottom padding after the last item to match the card’s comfortable feel. The left details card will usually be taller than the right report form card, so this panel extends downward, filling most of the vertical height of the page content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static panel within the left-column card. It scrolls with the page and vertically expands with the number of timeline events.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This loader is non-visual and is conceptually attached to the incident report form in the right column. It does not occupy any visual space; instead, it determines when the report form has enough configuration data to render. While configuration is loading, the right-column card may show a simple skeleton or spinner where the form would appear, but that visual representation belongs to the form component rather than the loader itself.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no explicit spatial presence. Influences loading states within the right-column form card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The incident report form is contained within a distinct card in the right-hand column, aligned so that the top edge of this card lines up horizontally with the top edge of the left-column incident details card. The right column occupies roughly one-third of the content width. The form card spans the full width of this right column, with 24–32px internal padding and a subtle border or shadow matching the left card. Within the card, a small heading (e.g., "Report this issue") appears at the top, followed by form fields arranged in a single vertical column with 12–16px gaps between inputs. The primary submit button sits at the bottom, aligned left. There is 24px of space between the card’s bottom internal padding and the last form control. Overall, the card’s height is shorter than the tall left card, leaving generous white space beneath it in the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card in the right column; scrolls with the page. Its width adapts with the responsive grid but maintains its 1/3 column proportion on desktop.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This submission handler is non-visual and is logically attached to the incident report form. It does not render any interface elements or occupy space on the page. Instead, it processes form submissions and triggers the display of the report confirmation panel or the error banner inside the same right-column card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no layout footprint. Influences state changes within the existing right-column card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation feedback panel appears within the same right-column card that houses the incident report form. When shown after a successful submission, it is positioned at the top of the card’s content area, replacing or pushing down the form fields. It uses a subtle success styling (e.g., green accent icon and title), with 16–24px internal vertical padding and full width inside the card. If the form remains visible, the confirmation sits above it with 16–24px of spacing, clearly indicating success while still allowing another submission. The card’s external size and position in the right column remain unchanged, preserving the overall layout. The confirmation panel’s text is concise, fitting comfortably within the existing card dimensions without requiring additional width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the form card that appears conditionally; scrolls with content and does not alter the card’s alignment relative to the left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The report error banner is a localized feedback element that appears inside the same right-column card as the incident report form, directly above the form fields. It spans the full width of the card’s inner area, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, and uses a strong error color accent. There is 8–12px of vertical space between the bottom of the banner and the first form field, keeping it visually related to the form but clearly separated. This banner does not cross into the left column; it remains contained within the card, maintaining the clean two-column separation from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, conditional block element within the form card; when visible, it pushes form content downward but does not affect the card’s placement or width.

name: About Help Center
title: About Help Center
description: This page retrieves and displays dynamically generated information about how to use the ecommerce dropshipping Help Center, and offers contextual navigation to key Help Center areas such as the home page and the support request form.

core_features: Fetch and render About Help Center content using the getAboutHelpCenterContent interface, including the page title and main explanatory body (HTML or markdown).

Render and manage a dynamic list of highlighted links from getAboutHelpCenterContent.highlighted_links, mapping each logical target (e.g., help_center_home, support_request_form) to the appropriate Help Center page.
Allow users to navigate from this page to the Help Center Home (index.html) via highlighted links whose target is mapped to the Help Center home route.
Allow users to navigate from this page directly to the Contact Support form (support_request.html) via highlighted links whose target is mapped to the support request route.

Optionally emphasize a specific highlighted link based on URL parameters so that users arriving from other pages can be guided to the most relevant action (for example, reading guidance then contacting support).
Optionally scroll to a specific section within the dynamically returned content using an anchor identifier provided via URL parameters, without relying on any hardcoded section IDs.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Learn what the Help Center is and return to Help Center home
1) User clicks the 'About Help Center' link in the global footer on any page.
2) The About Help Center page loads and its controller component immediately calls getAboutHelpCenterContent with no parameters.
3) While data is loading, the page shows a loading state managed by the controller (without hardcoded content).
4) When getAboutHelpCenterContent resolves, the controller passes the returned title and content to the content-display component, which renders the explanatory information about the Help Center.
5) The controller passes highlighted_links to the highlighted-links component; each link is rendered based on the label and target returned by the interface.
6) The user reads about the Help Center scope, global search, categories, status pages, and self-service guidance within the dynamic content.
7) The user clicks the highlighted link whose target indicates the Help Center home (e.g., target === 'help_center_home').
8) The click handler maps this logical target to the Help Center home route (index.html) and navigates there, preserving or augmenting URL parameters only if needed by the destination page.

Workflow 2: Decide whether to self-serve or contact support
1) User opens the About Help Center page (either from the footer or via a direct link from another article).
2) The controller fetches content via getAboutHelpCenterContent and renders it as in Workflow 1.
3) The user reads the guidance within the content about when to use self-service articles versus contacting support via the Support Request Form.
4) If the guidance suggests the situation is suitable for self-service, the user returns to the Help Center home or performs a search from elsewhere in the Help Center (outside this page).
5) If the guidance suggests direct support is appropriate (for example for billing downgrades or critical technical issues), the user clicks the highlighted link whose target indicates the support request form (e.g., target === 'support_request_form').
6) The click handler maps this logical target to the support request route (support_request.html) and navigates there, appending a URL parameter indicating that the user came from the About Help Center page using a page identifier rather than a hardcoded literal value.

Workflow 3: Deep-linked guidance with targeted emphasis
1) A user is reading another Help Center article or a status page and clicks a link leading to about_help_center.html with a query parameter that specifies a highlighted link to emphasize, for example: about_help_center.html?highlightTarget=support_request_form.
2) The About Help Center controller parses the URL on load and stores the highlightTarget value in page state.
3) The controller fetches content via getAboutHelpCenterContent and passes the resulting highlighted_links to the highlighted-links component along with the highlightTarget value.
4) The highlighted-links component compares each highlighted_links[i].target with the highlightTarget value. When there is a match, that link is marked as 'emphasized' in component state (for example, by setting a boolean flag used for styling and focus), without hardcoding which link is emphasized.
5) The user reads the content and is visually guided to the emphasized action (for example, contacting support) and clicks it to navigate onward.

Workflow 4: Deep-linked scroll to a specific section of the content
1) Another page links to about_help_center.html with a query parameter specifying a content anchor ID, for example: about_help_center.html?scrollTo=using-status-pages.
2) On load, the controller parses the scrollTo parameter and stores it in page state.
3) The controller calls getAboutHelpCenterContent and passes the content to the content-display component.
4) After the content-display component renders the HTML/markdown, the controller (or the content-display component via an emitted event) attempts to locate an element in the rendered content whose id matches the scrollTo parameter value.
5) If such an element is found, the page scrolls to that element (and may briefly emphasize it via styling controlled elsewhere); if not found, no additional behavior occurs.
6) The user reads the specific section they were deep-linked to, then optionally uses highlighted links to navigate to Help Center home or the support request form.
interactions: Page load: When about_help_center.html is loaded, the controller component parses the current URL's query parameters (e.g., highlightTarget and scrollTo) and then invokes getAboutHelpCenterContent once. It sets an internal loading state while awaiting the response.

Data load success: When getAboutHelpCenterContent resolves successfully, the controller updates its state with the returned title, content, and highlighted_links, clears any previous error state, and passes the data down to child components for rendering.

Data load error: If the getAboutHelpCenterContent call fails, the controller updates an error state flag and renders a generic error message or state using a global or shared pattern, without embedding any hardcoded error text specific to this page in the specification. Retry behavior (such as a 'Try again' button) can be implemented by re-invoking getAboutHelpCenterContent.

Render title and content: The content-display component receives the title and content fields from the controller and renders them without inserting any additional static phrases. If title is missing or empty, the component renders no heading; document title handling can be delegated to a global shell based on the same value.

Render highlighted links: The highlighted-links component receives the highlighted_links array from the controller. For each entry, it renders an interactive element whose visible label is bound directly to highlighted_links[i].label and whose logical target is bound to highlighted_links[i].target, with no hardcoded fallback labels.

Highlighted link emphasis: On initial render or whenever URL parameters change, the highlighted-links component compares the parsed highlightTarget value to each highlighted_links[i].target. If a match is found, the component marks that item as emphasized in its internal state, which can then be reflected visually (for example, by adding a CSS class) without embedding any specific styling details here.

Click on highlighted link to Help Center home: When a user clicks a highlighted link whose target indicates the Help Center home (as returned by the API), the click handler prevents default behavior if necessary and navigates to the Help Center home route (index.html). Additional query parameters may be appended or preserved according to global navigation rules, without relying on hardcoded labels or text.

Click on highlighted link to Support Request form: When a user clicks a highlighted link whose target indicates the Support Request form, the click handler navigates to support_request.html. It also appends a query parameter referencing the current page (for example, source=<page_identifier>) to populate SupportRequest.source_article_id or an equivalent field on the target page, using a value derived from the current route rather than a hardcoded literal in this specification.

Browser back/forward navigation: When the user uses the browser's back or forward buttons and the URL query string changes (for example, highlightTarget or scrollTo), the controller listens for these changes, re-parses the query parameters, updates its internal state, and triggers any necessary downstream updates (such as changing which link is emphasized or re-scrolling to a content anchor), without re-calling getAboutHelpCenterContent unless the route itself changes.

Scroll to content anchor: After the content-display component finishes rendering the content returned by getAboutHelpCenterContent, it emits an event or callback indicating that the DOM is ready for anchor-based scrolling. The controller then checks if a scrollTo parameter is present; if so, it attempts to find a matching element ID in the rendered content and programmatically scrolls to it.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The About Help Center page derives all of its dynamic content from getAboutHelpCenterContent and uses URL parameters (not localStorage) to manage view-specific state.

URL parameters:
- highlightTarget (optional): A string value representing a logical target identifier that should be emphasized in the list of highlighted links. This value is compared directly to each highlighted_links[i].target returned by getAboutHelpCenterContent. If highlightTarget matches a highlighted link's target, that link is marked in component state as emphasized. If highlightTarget is missing or does not match any target, no link is emphasized.

- scrollTo (optional): A string value representing the ID of an element within the rendered content (for example, a heading or section). After content is rendered from the content field returned by getAboutHelpCenterContent, the controller attempts to locate an element with id equal to scrollTo. If found, the page scrolls to it; otherwise, the parameter has no visible effect. This behavior is purely client-side and does not affect API calls.

- from (optional, pass-through): A string indicating the source route or context that led the user to this page (for example, an article slug or a status incident route). The About Help Center page does not change its UI based on this parameter but may include it unchanged when it navigates onward to other pages so downstream pages can use it for context (for example, pre-filling SupportRequest.source_article_id by mapping the source route to an article).

Data/state flow:
1) Initial state: On first load, the controller reads the current URL, extracts highlightTarget, scrollTo, and from (if present), and initializes its local state accordingly (with loading=true, error=false, and data=null).
2) Data fetch: The controller calls getAboutHelpCenterContent. The response is stored in state as aboutContent, including title, content, and highlighted_links.
3) Derived state: From aboutContent.highlighted_links and highlightTarget, the highlighted-links component computes an 'isEmphasized' boolean for each link. From aboutContent.content and scrollTo, the controller computes whether and where to scroll after render.
4) Subsequent navigation: When the user clicks a highlighted link, the click handler updates window.location to index.html or support_request.html with appropriate query parameters (including from and possibly source), ending the current page's lifecycle. No additional calls to getAboutHelpCenterContent are made during these navigations.
5) URL changes without full reload: If the application's routing system supports updating query parameters without a full page reload, the controller listens for such changes and re-applies the parameter parsing logic to update emphasis and scroll behavior without refetching content.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading for the About Help Center page, parses URL parameters for state (highlightTarget, scrollTo, from), orchestrates scrolling and emphasis behavior, and passes the getAboutHelpCenterContent response down to child components.

Receives the title and content fields returned by getAboutHelpCenterContent and renders them as the main informational body of the About Help Center page. It renders any HTML/markdown as provided and notifies the controller when rendering is complete so that scrollTo anchor behavior can be applied.

Renders an interactive list of highlighted links based on the highlighted_links array from getAboutHelpCenterContent. Each link displays its label as returned by the interface and navigates according to its logical target, with optional emphasis based on the highlightTarget URL parameter.

Encapsulates the mapping between highlighted_links[i].target values and actual routes. When triggered (typically via a click event from the highlighted-links component), it determines whether to navigate to the Help Center home (index.html), the Support Request form (support_request.html), or another route defined by the application, and appends appropriate query parameters such as source and from without relying on hardcoded display text.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container. On desktop, main article spans roughly 8 columns and the related-articles sidebar spans 4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a classic help-center detail layout. At the very top sits a full-width dark header containing the brand on the left and a large, horizontally centered search bar. Directly beneath the header, within a light background content area, a single breadcrumb row spans the full content width. Below that, a full-width H1 'About Help Center' title appears. Under the title, the main content splits into two columns: on the left, a large article card describing how to use the Help Center; on the right, a narrower card listing highlighted/related links. Contextual navigation elements (home, support request) are integrated with the breadcrumb and within the article area.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a classic help-center detail layout. At the very top sits a full-width dark header containing the brand on the left and a large, horizontally centered search bar. Directly beneath the header, within a light background content area, a single breadcrumb row spans the full content width. Below that, a full-width H1 'About Help Center' title appears. Under the title, the main content splits into two columns: on the left, a large article card describing how to use the Help Center; on the right, a narrower card listing highlighted/related links. Contextual navigation elements (home, support request) are integrated with the breadcrumb and within the article area.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The dark header occupies roughly 15–20% of the viewport height, visually dominated by the search field. The main content sits in a centered container that spans about 80–88% of the viewport width, capped at a max-width around 1120–1200px, leaving generous margins on very wide screens. Inside this container, the left article card takes approximately two-thirds (about 8 of 12 grid columns), and the right highlighted-links card takes about one-third (about 4 of 12 columns). Vertically, the article card is the tallest element, while the sidebar card is shorter, leaving intentional whitespace beneath it. Section gaps follow 24–40px vertical increments, and card paddings follow the 24–32px internal padding pattern.
layout_strategies.content_density: Text and interactive elements are arranged with a moderately spacious density: no clutter, but not sparse. Lines of text are comfortably spaced, paragraphs have small 8–16px gaps, and lists are single-column and easy to scan. Cards use 24–32px padding on all sides, while the search bar and breadcrumb have tighter, functional spacing to reinforce a structured, corporate feel. Overall, content feels readable and focused, with clear separation between structural regions.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts with the dark header and large, central search bar as the primary attention grabber. From there, the user’s eye moves down to the breadcrumb at the upper left of the content area, then to the H1 page title. After reading the title, attention flows naturally into the left article card (primary content), moving top-to-bottom through intro text and any step-by-step guidance. The right highlighted-links card functions as a secondary focal area, discovered peripherally as users scan from left to right. Navigation elements at the top (breadcrumb) and near the end of the article provide clear next steps without breaking the primary reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a modern, corporate help-center layout: a dark, full-width top bar anchors the experience, with the brand name aligned to the left and a prominent search bar centered horizontally and vertically within that header band. Below, on a light background, a centered content container holds the main help-center information. The first row is a left-aligned breadcrumb showing the user’s position within the Help Center and offering quick navigation back to key areas. Directly underneath, a large, clear H1 heading, such as 'About the Help Center', spans the full container width and sets the page context. Below the heading, the layout splits into two columns. On the left, a spacious card extends across roughly two-thirds of the container, housing the dynamically generated explanation of how to use the Help Center, including introductory copy and any step-by-step guidance. On the right, a shorter card occupies the remaining one-third, labeled with a heading like 'Highlighted Areas' or 'Key Help Center Links', and contains a vertical list of links to important sections like Help Center home, support request forms, or category overviews. The card on the left is taller and drives the main reading experience, while the right card leaves some whitespace beneath it, giving the layout breathing room. Breadcrumb navigation at the top and contextual links within or below the left card work together to guide users, all within a neat, card-based structure that feels consistent, minimal, and easy to scan.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller governs the entire canvas layout. It establishes a full-viewport-height page with a fixed dark header at the very top and a scrolling content area beneath. The header spans 100% of the viewport width and about 15–20% of the viewport height on desktop, using a dark background. Inside this header, the brand logo or name is anchored at the far left with 24–32px horizontal padding from the viewport edge, while a large search bar is horizontally centered and stretches across roughly 50–60% of the viewport width. Vertical padding in the header (24–32px) keeps the search bar visually dominant but not cramped. Below this header, the controller positions a light-background main content section. This section is centered and constrained to a maximum width of around 1120–1200px, with automatic left/right margins so that on large screens it visually occupies about 80–88% of the screen width. Within this section, the controller defines a 12-column responsive grid: on desktop, it reserves the full width row just under the header for the breadcrumb, then a full-width row for the H1 title, and then creates a two-column row for the main cards. The left column spans approximately 8 of 12 columns (around 66% of the container width) for the article card, and the right column spans 4 of 12 columns (around 34%) for the highlighted-links card. Vertical gaps between header, breadcrumb, title, and cards follow a rhythm of 24–40px (roughly 3–5 multiples of the 8px base unit). On tablet and mobile (below about 1024px), the controller stacks the columns so that the article card appears first and spans 100% width, followed by the highlighted-links card spanning 100% width, maintaining 24px vertical separation between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The controller enforces a fixed-position top header while the main content area scrolls beneath it. It manages responsive behavior by shifting the two-column layout into a single-column vertical stack on smaller viewports and ensures consistent 8px-based spacing throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content-display component appears in the main content area as the dominant left-hand article card. It is positioned below the H1 title, aligned to the left edge of the centered content container. On desktop, it spans approximately two-thirds of the container width (about 8 out of 12 grid columns) and occupies the full available height required by its content. There is a horizontal gap of about 24–32px between its right edge and the adjacent highlighted-links card. The card itself has a subtle elevation or border to separate it from the background and uses internal padding of 24–32px on all sides. At the top inside the card, the introductory text about the Help Center is displayed, followed by a clear subheading (for example, 'How This Help Center Works' or 'Step-by-Step Guide') separated by about 16–24px from the intro. Below this, any stepwise explanations or numbered lists are stacked vertically, with each step separated by 8–16px to keep the list readable but compact. Line lengths are controlled by the card width so that text remains comfortable to read. Near the bottom of the card, below the main explanatory content, any contextual navigation items (for example, inline links to 'Help Center Home' or 'Submit a Support Request') are placed in a small block with 16–24px top margin from the body text, either as a short vertical list or a row of links. The bottom of the card sits above the page footer with a vertical buffer of about 48–64px, continuing the moderately spacious feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The content-display card behaves as a standard block element within the grid: it scrolls with the page and maintains its two-thirds width on desktop. On smaller screens it reflows to full-width, stacking above the highlighted-links card while preserving its internal padding and vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlighted-links component is rendered as a secondary card on the right side of the main content row. On desktop, it is top-aligned with the content-display card so that their top edges line up horizontally under the H1. It spans roughly one-third of the centered container width (about 4 of 12 columns) and sits to the right of the article card, separated by a 24–32px horizontal gap. The card’s height is determined by its content and is intentionally shorter than the article card, leaving a band of clean whitespace below it within the main content section. Internally, the card uses 24–32px padding. At the top inside the card, a concise heading such as 'Highlighted Links', 'Key Help Center Areas', or 'Related Articles' is placed, aligned to the left, with a 16–24px gap below it before the list of links begins. The links are displayed as a vertical list: each link is on its own line, left-aligned, with 8–12px vertical spacing between items. The list is compact but readable, designed to be easily scannable. If link descriptions or secondary text are used, they are set in a smaller font beneath each link, with reduced spacing to maintain overall density. On tablet and mobile, this card collapses into a full-width block positioned directly below the main article card, with 24px of vertical spacing in between to preserve a clear separation while keeping the relationship obvious.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This component is a non-sticky, right-column card on larger screens and a standard full-width block on smaller screens. It scrolls normally with the page and reflows below the article when the viewport is too narrow for two columns.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-mapper component controls and renders contextual navigation elements both above and within the main content. Its primary visible manifestation is the breadcrumb row placed directly beneath the fixed header, at the top of the light-background content area. This breadcrumb runs horizontally across the full width of the centered content container, left-aligned with the article card. It is anchored with 24–32px of vertical spacing below the bottom edge of the header and about 8–16px above the H1 title. The breadcrumb items themselves are in a single line, with subtle separators (such as '>' or slashes) and 8–12px spacing between items, maintaining a compact but legible density. Secondarily, the navigation-mapper also injects contextual navigation links into or just beneath the main article content inside the content-display card. These links appear in a small block near the bottom of the card—after the main explanatory text and any step-by-step list—with a 16–24px top margin from the preceding text. They may appear as a short vertical stack of text links or as a tight row of buttons/text links (for example: 'Go to Help Center Home', 'Browse Categories', 'Submit a Support Request'), aligned to the left to maintain a consistent reading flow. On narrow screens, both the breadcrumb and these in-card navigation elements stay left-aligned and stack naturally, ensuring that the user’s path to key Help Center areas is always visually clear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The breadcrumb row scrolls with the page and sits directly under the fixed header without being sticky itself. The in-article navigation links are embedded at the bottom of the content-display card and move as part of that card’s content. The component adapts to single-column layouts on smaller screens by preserving left alignment and vertical stacking of navigation elements.

name: Terms of Use
title: Terms of Use
description: Displays the dynamically loaded Terms of Use for the Help Center and provides deep-link navigation to specific sections.

core_features: Fetch and display the latest Terms of Use content using the getTermsOfUseContent interface on page load.
Show the Terms of Use title and last updated date returned by the interface.
Render the full Terms of Use body content returned by the interface, preserving its HTML/markdown structure.
Support deep linking to specific sections within the Terms of Use via a URL parameter and in-page anchors.
Display loading and error states for the Terms of Use fetch, with a user-triggered retry option on failure.
Allow users to copy a direct link to the current section of the Terms of Use by updating the URL parameters without requiring a page reload.
user_workflows: Viewing the Terms of Use from the footer link: 1) User clicks the 'Terms of use' link in the footer (navigates to terms.html). 2) The page initializes and triggers getTermsOfUseContent. 3) While loading, a loading state is shown instead of content. 4) On success, the page displays the title, last updated date, and full Terms of Use content. 5) The user scrolls through the content or uses any generated section navigation (if available) to read specific parts.

Opening a direct deep link to a specific section of the Terms of Use: 1) User navigates to a URL such as terms.html?section={sectionId}. 2) The page initializes and triggers getTermsOfUseContent. 3) After content is successfully loaded and rendered, the page looks for an element whose identifier matches the section parameter value. 4) If found, the page scrolls to that section and marks it as active (e.g., in the section navigation). 5) If no matching section is found, the page remains at the top but keeps the URL parameter unchanged.

Handling an error when loading the Terms of Use: 1) User navigates to terms.html. 2) The page calls getTermsOfUseContent. 3) The interface call fails (e.g., network or server error). 4) The page hides the normal content area and shows an error state message with a retry action. 5) User clicks the retry action. 6) The page triggers getTermsOfUseContent again and, on success, replaces the error state with the loaded title, last updated date, and content.

Copying a direct link to a specific Terms of Use section: 1) User scrolls within the Terms of Use content. 2) User clicks on a section anchor control associated with a specific heading. 3) The page updates the browser URL to include or replace the section URL parameter with the section’s identifier (e.g., terms.html?section={sectionId}) using navigation state mechanics. 4) The page keeps the user at the current scroll position and optionally visually indicates that the section link has been copied or is active. 5) The user can now copy the URL from the browser address bar and share it as a direct link.
interactions: On page initialization, trigger a fetch of Terms of Use data via getTermsOfUseContent with no parameters.
On successful data fetch, bind the returned title to the page title component, last_updated to the last-updated indicator, and content to the main content renderer.
On data fetch error, set an error state in the page and display an error message along with a retry control.
On click of the retry control, re-trigger the getTermsOfUseContent call and reset the loading state.
On initial load, read the section URL parameter (if present) and, after content rendering, scroll to and mark as active the element that matches this section identifier.
On click of any in-page section anchor control, update the URL section parameter to the associated section identifier and optionally adjust the active section state, without reloading the page.
On browser navigation events that change the section parameter (e.g., back/forward navigation), re-apply the active section and scroll behavior based on the new parameter value.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page reads and manages state primarily through URL parameters and interface results. On load, the page does not require any URL parameters but optionally accepts 'section' as a query parameter (e.g., terms.html?section={sectionId}). Internal page state includes: (1) loadingState: 'idle' | 'loading' | 'success' | 'error'; (2) termsData: the object returned from getTermsOfUseContent containing title, last_updated, and content; and (3) activeSectionId: derived from the URL 'section' parameter or from the last clicked section anchor. Workflow: When the page loads, loadingState is set to 'loading' and getTermsOfUseContent is called. On success, loadingState becomes 'success', termsData is stored, and the content is rendered. After rendering, if a 'section' parameter exists, the page searches the rendered content for a section whose identifier matches the parameter and scrolls to it; activeSectionId is set to this value. If the 'section' parameter is absent or does not match any element, activeSectionId is left null and no scrolling adjustment is made beyond the default.
