On error from getTermsOfUseContent, loadingState becomes 'error' and the error UI is shown. Clicking the retry button sets loadingState back to 'loading' and re-invokes getTermsOfUseContent. When a user clicks a section anchor in the content, the page updates the 'section' URL parameter to that section’s identifier and sets activeSectionId accordingly, without reloading the page; this maintains alignment between UI and URL state. If the browser back/forward navigation changes the 'section' parameter, the page listens for URL changes and updates activeSectionId and scroll position to match the new parameter value.

components.functionality: Coordinates the overall Terms of Use page behavior: triggers getTermsOfUseContent on initialization, manages loading/success/error states, and synchronizes URL parameters with internal state.
Displays a loading state whenever the Terms of Use content is being fetched and hides itself once the fetch completes or fails.
Shows an error message and a retry action when getTermsOfUseContent fails, and hides itself when the content is successfully loaded.
Displays the Terms of Use title and last updated information using the data returned from getTermsOfUseContent.
Renders the full Terms of Use body using the 'content' field returned by getTermsOfUseContent, preserving its HTML or markdown structure, and exposes identifiable sections for deep linking.

Optionally builds an in-page section navigation based on the structure of the Terms of Use content (e.g., headings), tracks the active section based on scroll position and URL 'section' parameter, and allows users to click items to jump to specific sections.
Reads and updates the 'section' URL parameter to keep deep links to specific sections in sync with the user’s current position or selection within the Terms of Use content.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container. On desktop, main article spans roughly 8 columns and the related-articles sidebar spans 4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a vertical flow that mirrors a modern support-center article page: a full-width dark header bar at the top containing brand and a prominent, centered search field; below that, a light-background main area with a breadcrumb + page meta row aligned to the left inside a centered content container, followed by a large H1 for 'Terms of Use'. Under the H1, split the main region into two columns: the left column is the primary terms content card, the right column is a secondary in-page section navigation card. Loading and error states occupy the same main-content zone as the article, while URL deeplink behavior is invisible but controls how the page scrolls to sections.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a vertical flow that mirrors a modern support-center article page: a full-width dark header bar at the top containing brand and a prominent, centered search field; below that, a light-background main area with a breadcrumb + page meta row aligned to the left inside a centered content container, followed by a large H1 for 'Terms of Use'. Under the H1, split the main region into two columns: the left column is the primary terms content card, the right column is a secondary in-page section navigation card. Loading and error states occupy the same main-content zone as the article, while URL deeplink behavior is invisible but controls how the page scrolls to sections.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve the top ~15–20% of the viewport height for the dark header and search, spanning 100% width. Below, use a centered max-width container (roughly 1080–1200px) with generous left/right margins (~15–20% of viewport on large screens). Inside this container, give the left article card roughly two-thirds of the available width and the right section-navigation card about one-third. Vertically, maintain 24–40px gaps between header, breadcrumb/meta row, H1, and the two-column card area. The terms content card is the tallest element, while the section navigation card is shorter, leaving intentional whitespace under it.
layout_strategies.content_density: Apply a moderately spacious density: keep clear separation between structural blocks (header, breadcrumb/meta, title, card region) using 24–32px vertical spacing, and within cards use 24–32px padding on all sides. Text lines should be comfortably spaced with clear paragraph separation. Lists of sections or terms are single-column for easy scanning; the in-page navigation list remains compact but not cramped, with 8–12px vertical spacing between links.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: First draw attention to the dark header and prominent centered search bar. From there, the eye moves down to the breadcrumb/meta row at the upper-left of the content container, then to the large H1 'Terms of Use'. After reading the title, the gaze naturally follows into the main two-column zone, prioritizing the larger left card that holds the terms content while treating the right navigation card as supporting context for quick jumps. Within the terms content, the reading path is strongly top-down through sections, while the section navigation on the right provides lateral, glanceable access to specific anchors.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, modern help-center article view. A full-width, dark header band sits at the very top, fixed to the top edge, with the brand aligned to the left and a wide, horizontally centered search bar that dominates the header visually. Below this, the background switches to a light tone and a centered content container appears, leaving wide left and right gutters. At the top of this container, a single-row breadcrumb or page meta line runs near the left edge, followed by a generous gap and a prominent H1 reading 'Terms of Use'. Beneath the title, the layout splits into two columns: on the left, a large, light card with subtle shadow contains the full terms document, scrolling vertically; on the right, a narrower card lists section titles as links for deep-link navigation. The left card occupies roughly two-thirds of the container width and stretches down the page, while the right navigation card takes about one-third and ends sooner, leaving clean whitespace beneath it. Loading and error states temporarily replace the left card region while preserving the overall page structure, and URL deeplink behavior ensures that selecting a section in the right card smoothly scrolls the left content card to the corresponding section. The overall effect is structured, corporate, and easy to scan, with a strong top-down reading path anchored by the header and title and a subtle left-right relationship between main content and sectional navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-page-controller is an invisible, full-page wrapper that spans 100% of the viewport width and height, sitting at the root of the page. It establishes the vertical stacking order from top to bottom: first the full-width dark header, then the light-background main content region with the centered content container, inside which the breadcrumb/meta row, H1 title, and the two-column card layout (terms content on the left, navigation on the right) are rendered. The controller determines which visual state is shown in the main content region—either the loading-indicator, the error-panel, or the normal combination of terms-content-view and terms-section-navigation—without altering the header’s position or proportions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural wrapper that occupies the entire page canvas; it controls which child components are mounted in the content area but does not visibly alter spacing beyond orchestrating standard stacking and responsive behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the terms-loading-indicator appears in the main content zone below the header, centered within the same max-width container where the terms content card would normally appear. It is positioned roughly where the left content card starts: aligned to the left column’s horizontal starting point but visually centered within that column’s width. Vertically, it sits with 24–32px of whitespace below the H1 title, matching the spacing that would be used before the cards. The indicator itself should not span the full width; instead, it uses a compact width (about 40–50% of the content container or matching the width of the left card) and is surrounded by generous padding and whitespace so that it feels like a temporary placeholder. The right-hand section navigation column is either hidden or visually de-emphasized while loading, keeping the user’s focus on the central loading indicator region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in place of the terms-content-view within the main left column and vertically pushes any other content below it; it does not scroll independently and disappears once the content has loaded, restoring the normal two-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-error-panel occupies the same visual territory as the main article card within the centered content container. It is aligned to the left column start, spanning roughly two-thirds of the container width, and is presented as a card with similar padding (24–32px) and vertical spacing as the normal content card. The panel begins approximately 24–32px below the H1 title, maintaining the established structural rhythm. It may include an error icon, message, and retry action stacked vertically, with comfortable spacing between elements. The right section-navigation column is hidden or pushed below the error card to avoid implying that navigation is available when the content is not. Whitespace surrounds the error card on the right (toward the empty sidebar zone) and below, preserving the clean, spacious feel even in error state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the terms-content-view in the left column when an error occurs. It scrolls with the page as a normal block-level card and returns control to the standard layout when dismissed or once data loads successfully.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-header is a full-width, horizontally spanning bar fixed to the very top of the page, occupying roughly 15–20% of the viewport height on initial load. It uses a dark background color, clearly separating it from the light main content below. Inside, content is constrained to the same max-width centered container used in the main area. On the left side of this container, the brand mark or site name is aligned flush with the container’s left edge with about 16–24px padding to the top and bottom. Dominating the header visually, a long search field is horizontally centered within the container and extends to about 60–70% of the container width. The search field has generous vertical padding (approximately 12–16px) and horizontal padding (16–24px), giving it substantial presence. There is ample vertical space above and below the search field within the header so that it feels unconstrained and prominent. The bottom edge of the header aligns sharply with the beginning of the light main-content background below, creating a clear horizontal breakpoint that anchors the rest of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the top of the viewport so it remains visible as the user scrolls the terms content. The internal elements (brand and search) are centered within a max-width container; the header itself does not scroll, while all main content beneath it scrolls vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-content-view is rendered as the primary left-side card within the main content area. Below the fixed header, the main light-background region begins; inside this region, a centered max-width container hosts a breadcrumb/meta row at the top-left, followed by a prominent H1 'Terms of Use'. Approximately 24–32px beneath the H1, the terms-content-view starts as a card aligned to the container’s left edge. This card occupies roughly two-thirds of the container width, leaving about one-third of the width to its right for the section navigation card, separated by a 24–32px horizontal gap. The card has 24–32px internal padding on all sides. The terms content itself flows vertically: a brief introductory paragraph near the top, followed by multiple sections with clear headings and paragraphs. Section headings are visually distinct to support deep linking, and the overall document can extend well below the viewport, creating a tall scrolling column. The card’s right edge lines up visually with the inner edge of the right navigation card, reinforcing the two-column grid. A modest shadow or subtle border separates the card from the background, while balanced top and bottom padding maintain the moderately spacious feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a scrollable main column integrated into the page flow; it scrolls under the fixed header. It remains fixed in width relative to the content container on larger screens and collapses to full width above the section-navigation card on smaller breakpoints (e.g., stacking vertically with navigation below). It works closely with the deeplink manager to allow scrolling directly to anchored sections within this card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-section-navigation appears to the right of the main terms-content-view within the same centered container, beginning at roughly the same vertical position as the top of the terms content card (about 24–32px below the H1). It is rendered as a narrower card that occupies roughly one-third of the container width, with a 24–32px horizontal gap separating it from the left content card. The top of this card includes a small, clear heading such as 'On this page' or 'Sections', followed by a vertically stacked list of section titles or anchors corresponding to the main terms content. Each link sits on its own line with about 8–12px vertical spacing between items, creating an easily scannable list without feeling cramped. The card’s internal padding mirrors the left card’s (24–32px) for consistency. Vertically, the navigation card is shorter than the full content card and typically ends higher on the page, leaving deliberate whitespace beneath it before the bottom of the viewport or before any footer elements. This reinforces its role as a secondary sidebar rather than competing with the main content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger viewports, the navigation card behaves like a sidebar and can adopt a sticky behavior: after the user scrolls past its initial position, it remains affixed within the right column with a small top offset below the fixed header, so its links remain accessible. On narrower screens, it stacks below the terms-content-view as a full-width card, losing its sidebar appearance and sticky behavior while maintaining clear vertical separation from the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-deeplink-manager is an invisible behavioral layer that conceptually sits alongside the terms-content-view and terms-section-navigation within the page structure. It does not occupy visible space or alter the visual arrangement, but it tracks the URL’s hash or query parameters and maps them to section anchors within the left content card. When the page loads with a specific anchor or when a user clicks a link in the right section-navigation card, the deeplink manager smoothly scrolls the main terms-content-view so that the targeted section heading aligns comfortably below the fixed header, ensuring it is clearly visible. It also may highlight the corresponding navigation item in the right card by applying an active state, but this highlighting does not change the underlying card dimensions or spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and overlay-like in behavior; it does not claim layout space but orchestrates scrolling and in-page focus. Its only spatial effect is indirect: it adjusts scroll position within the main document so targeted sections sit with appropriate offset beneath the fixed header and within the visible area of the viewport.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Page that dynamically loads and displays the Help Center privacy policy, including data collection, usage, and user rights, using the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface.

core_features: Fetch and display the full Privacy Policy content (title, last updated date, and body) using the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface when the page is loaded.
Render the Privacy Policy body (HTML or markdown) in a scrollable content area without hardcoded sections, relying entirely on the content returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent.
Optionally generate a table of contents (TOC) based on headings detected in the returned Privacy Policy content to help users navigate long policies.
Support deep linking to specific sections of the Privacy Policy via URL parameters (e.g., privacy.html?section=...) by scrolling to the matching section after content is rendered.
Allow users to share direct links to specific sections of the Privacy Policy by updating the URL parameters when they click section links and providing a copy-link interaction.
React to browser navigation changes (back/forward) by re-applying the section URL parameter logic (scrolling/highlighting) without reloading the policy from the server unnecessarily.
Serve as a stable reference page that can be opened from the site footer or from other Help Center pages using a simple URL, while always showing the latest content from getPrivacyPolicyContent.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View the Privacy Policy from the footer
1. User clicks the 'Privacy policy' link in the global footer.
2. Browser navigates to privacy.html with no additional URL parameters.
3. On page load, the controller component invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent.
4. While waiting, a generic loading state is shown in place of the content.
5. When getPrivacyPolicyContent returns, the page header displays the returned title and last_updated values.
6. The content viewer renders the returned content field as HTML/markdown.
7. If headings are detected in the content, the TOC component is shown; otherwise, it remains hidden.
8. User scrolls through the policy; the URL remains privacy.html unless section-specific navigation is used.

Workflow: Open a direct link to a specific Privacy Policy section
1. User clicks a link from another page (e.g., a support article) that points to privacy.html with a section URL parameter (for example, privacy.html?section=someSectionId). The exact value is controlled by the linking page.
2. Browser loads privacy.html and the controller reads the section parameter from the URL.
3. The controller invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent.
4. After the content is rendered, the section-deeplink handler attempts to locate an element in the rendered content whose identifier matches the section parameter.
5. If a matching element is found, the page scrolls that element into view.
6. The user can read the targeted section; scrolling beyond it does not modify the section parameter unless they explicitly click a different section link.

Workflow: Navigate between sections using the table of contents
1. User is viewing the Privacy Policy and the TOC is visible because headings were detected in the content.
2. User clicks a TOC entry corresponding to a section.
3. The TOC click handler requests the content viewer to scroll to the associated heading element.
4. The URL is updated using history APIs to set or replace the section URL parameter with the identifier of the selected heading, without reloading the page.
5. If the user clicks another TOC entry, the same process repeats and the section parameter value is updated.
6. If the user copies the page URL after selecting a section, the URL includes the section parameter so that others opening it land directly on that section.

Workflow: Share a direct link to a currently viewed section
1. User scrolls to a section of the Privacy Policy and activates a section-level link control (for example, a section link icon or menu attached to a heading).
2. The section link handler computes the corresponding section identifier for that heading.
3. The handler updates the URL to include or replace the section parameter with that identifier.
4. The handler copies the current page URL (with the section parameter) to the clipboard using client-side clipboard functionality.
5. User pastes this URL elsewhere (email, chat, documentation). When another person opens this URL, the 'Open a direct link to a specific Privacy Policy section' workflow applies.

Workflow: Handle loading errors when fetching the Privacy Policy
1. User navigates to privacy.html.
2. The controller invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. If the interface call fails (for example, network or server error), the controller enters an error state.
4. The page displays a generic error message and a retry control instead of the policy content.
5. User clicks the retry control.
6. The controller re-invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent and, if successful, exits the error state and displays the content as in the standard viewing workflow.
interactions: On page load, the controller automatically calls getPrivacyPolicyContent with no parameters to retrieve the latest policy data.
While getPrivacyPolicyContent is pending, the content viewer shows a non-blocking loading indicator instead of policy text.
If getPrivacyPolicyContent returns successfully, the header component binds to the returned title and last_updated fields, and the content viewer binds to and renders the returned content field.
If the returned content contains recognizable heading elements (as defined by the rendering logic), the TOC component extracts their labels and identifiers to build a clickable list of sections.
When a user clicks a TOC item, the TOC click handler scrolls the content viewer to the corresponding heading and updates the URL's section parameter to match that heading's identifier.

When a user activates a section-level link control (such as a link icon next to a heading), the section deeplink handler updates the section parameter in the URL and copies the resulting URL to the clipboard.

When the browser's back/forward buttons change the URL (including section parameter changes), the controller listens to URL change events and instructs the content viewer to scroll to the section referenced by the current section parameter if present.

When a loading error occurs, the error state displays a generic error message and a retry control; clicking the retry control re-triggers the getPrivacyPolicyContent call and hides the error state if the next call succeeds.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Privacy Policy page uses URL query parameters to control in-page navigation state without additional API calls. The base URL is privacy.html. An optional section parameter (privacy.html?section=...) is used to target specific sections of the dynamically loaded policy. On initial page load, the controller parses the current URL. It always calls getPrivacyPolicyContent once and stores the result in page state. After the content is rendered, if a section parameter is present, the section-deeplink handler attempts to scroll to the element whose identifier matches the section value. If no matching element exists, the page remains at the top of the content. When users navigate via the TOC or section-level link controls, the page updates the section parameter in the URL using browser history APIs without reloading the page. This ensures that the current section focus is encoded in the URL and can be shared or recovered via browser navigation. Browser back/forward events are listened for; when the URL changes, the controller re-applies the section parameter logic to scroll to the appropriate section using the already-loaded content, avoiding repeated calls to getPrivacyPolicyContent. No other URL parameters are interpreted by this page, and no localStorage or extra client-side persistence mechanisms are used.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, error handling, and communication between the header, TOC, and content viewer components for the Privacy Policy page.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse the current URL, call getPrivacyPolicyContent, manage loading and error states, and pass the response to child components once available.
onUrlChange: Listen for browser navigation events and, when the section parameter changes, instruct the content viewer to scroll to the referenced section.
onRetryLoad: Triggered by the error component; re-invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent and resets the error state if successful.
onContentReady: Triggered by the content viewer when it has finished rendering; used by the controller to apply initial section scrolling based on the section URL parameter.

components.functionality: Displays the Privacy Policy page heading and last updated information using values returned from getPrivacyPolicyContent, without hardcoding text or dates.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When the controller provides new policy data, update the displayed title and last updated value.

components.functionality: Renders the Privacy Policy body returned in the content field from getPrivacyPolicyContent, supports scrolling to specific sections by identifier, and emits events when rendering completes.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When new content is provided by the controller, render the HTML/markdown into the content area and notify the controller when finished.
onScrollToSection(sectionId): Scroll the rendered content to the element whose identifier matches sectionId, if such an element exists.
onRenderComplete: After content has been rendered, notify the controller so it can trigger initial section scrolling based on URL parameters.
onHeadingMetadataExtracted(headingList): When heading metadata (labels and identifiers) is extracted from the rendered content, provide this list to the TOC component via the controller.

components.functionality: Generates and displays a table of contents for the Privacy Policy using heading metadata extracted from the rendered content, and allows users to navigate between sections.
event_handlers: onHeadingsUpdate(headingList): When the content viewer provides a new list of headings, update the TOC entries and show or hide the TOC depending on whether any headings are present.
onClickTocItem(sectionId): When a user clicks a TOC entry, instruct the controller to scroll to the corresponding section and update the section parameter in the URL.
onActiveSectionChange(sectionId): Optionally highlight the currently active section in the TOC when informed by the controller that the active section has changed.

components.functionality: Manages synchronization between section-focused navigation and the URL's section parameter, enabling deep links to specific parts of the Privacy Policy.
event_handlers: onInitialUrlParsed(sectionId): When the page first loads and the URL is parsed, request the content viewer to scroll to sectionId after rendering completes.
onSectionFocusChange(sectionId): When a user navigates to a new section (via TOC or section link), update the URL's section parameter using history APIs without reloading the page.
onBrowserNavigation(sectionId): When the user uses browser back/forward and the section parameter changes, request the content viewer to scroll to the section indicated by the new sectionId.
onCopySectionLink(sectionId): Construct the current page URL with the provided sectionId as the section parameter and copy it to the clipboard for sharing.

components.functionality: Displays a generic error state when getPrivacyPolicyContent fails and provides a way for the user to retry loading the Privacy Policy.
event_handlers: onShowError: Triggered by the controller when the getPrivacyPolicyContent call fails; renders an error message and a retry control.
onHideError: Triggered by the controller when a new load attempt starts or succeeds; hides the error display.
onClickRetry: When the user activates the retry control, notify the controller to re-invoke getPrivacyPolicyContent.

components.functionality: Shows or hides a loading state while the Privacy Policy data is being fetched using getPrivacyPolicyContent.
event_handlers: onLoadingStateChange(isLoading): When the controller's loading state changes, show or hide the loading indicator accordingly.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container. On desktop, main article spans roughly 8 columns and the related-articles sidebar spans 4 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom structure that mirrors the support-center mockup. Above this page, the global site shell provides the dark header with brand and search. Within the help-center content area, the privacy-policy-header spans the full width of the centered content column at the top, followed by the main content region split into two columns: the left column is the privacy-policy-content-viewer (the full policy text), and the right column is the privacy-policy-toc (a navigational card listing policy sections). The controller, deeplink handler, loading indicator, and error state do not introduce new structural sections but instead control or overlay this main structure as needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom structure that mirrors the support-center mockup. Above this page, the global site shell provides the dark header with brand and search. Within the help-center content area, the privacy-policy-header spans the full width of the centered content column at the top, followed by the main content region split into two columns: the left column is the privacy-policy-content-viewer (the full policy text), and the right column is the privacy-policy-toc (a navigational card listing policy sections). The controller, deeplink handler, loading indicator, and error state do not introduce new structural sections but instead control or overlay this main structure as needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is constrained to a centered max-width container (roughly 1120–1200px) with generous left and right margins (about 15–20% of the viewport each on large screens). Within this container, the privacy-policy-header takes a single row at the top. Below it, the two-column layout assigns roughly two-thirds of the content width (about 8 of 12 grid columns) to the policy-content-viewer on the left and one-third (about 4 of 12 columns) to the policy-toc on the right. Vertical spacing between structural sections (header, then main two-column region) follows increments of 24–40px. Cards (content viewer and TOC) have 24–32px internal padding on all sides, consistent with the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density is moderately spacious. The header area has clear separation between any breadcrumb/meta text and the H1. Inside the content viewer, paragraphs are set with comfortable line spacing and 16–24px spacing between sections. The TOC card uses tight but readable spacing between links so the list is scannable without feeling cramped. Overall, elements breathe but the long-form policy text is still presented efficiently, avoiding excessive whitespace that would require unnecessary scrolling.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the global dark header and search (site shell), then drops into the privacy-policy-header, where the H1 'Privacy Policy' is the primary focal point. From there, the main reading path flows down into the large left card (privacy-policy-content-viewer), which dominates attention. The right column TOC card sits slightly to the right and is visually secondary, acting as a navigational aid users glance at to jump between sections. As users scroll, the left column provides a strong top-down reading flow through sections of the policy; the right TOC remains consistently accessible (with sticky behavior on desktop) to support quick navigation and reinforce orientation within the document.

layout.overall_description: Inside the light-background help center area beneath the global dark header, the privacy policy page is framed within a centered, max-width container. At the top of this container, a full-width header band introduces the page: a subtle breadcrumb or contextual label sits at the upper left, with a large, bold 'Privacy Policy' H1 beneath it and a short line of meta information (such as ‘Last updated’ or a one-sentence description) aligned left. Generous vertical spacing (around 32px) separates this header from the main body. Below, the layout divides into two columns: on the left, a tall white card spans roughly two-thirds of the content width, holding the full policy text with clearly separated sections and headings. On the right, a shorter white card occupies about one-third of the width, titled ‘On this page’ or ‘Contents’ and listing the main policy sections as vertical links. The right card aligns to the top of the left card and is significantly shorter, leaving comfortable whitespace below it while the policy content continues down. On desktop, the TOC card can remain sticky, hovering within the right column as the user scrolls the long policy on the left. When loading or errors occur, the content area is replaced or overlaid by a centered indicator or message, but the overall structure of a header above a two-column content region remains conceptually intact. The result is a calm, corporate, knowledge-base style layout that emphasizes clarity, readability, and straightforward navigation through the policy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-page-controller is a non-visual orchestration layer that sits conceptually around the entire privacy policy page content. It controls when the header, content viewer, TOC, loading indicator, and error state are rendered, but it does not render any visible UI itself. Spatially, it introduces no padding, margins, or dimensions of its own; it simply manages which visible components occupy the main content container beneath the global dark header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Has no visual representation and occupies no space in the layout; it only affects which other components are mounted within the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-header is positioned at the top of the page’s main content container, immediately below the global site header and search region. It spans the full width of the centered content column (roughly 80–70% of the viewport width, capped at about 1120–1200px), aligned to the left edge of that container. There is a vertical offset of approximately 32–40px from the bottom of the dark global header to the top of this component, creating clear separation between the site shell and the help center page. Within the header, a slim breadcrumb or contextual label row sits at the top-left, aligned with the main content grid, followed by a large H1 title ‘Privacy Policy’ below it, left-aligned. Optionally, a short single-line description or ‘Last updated’ meta text appears beneath the H1, also left-aligned. Internal vertical spacing follows the 8px base scale: about 8–12px between breadcrumb and H1, and 8–16px between H1 and description. The bottom of the header has 24–32px margin before the two-column card area begins, visually separating the header from the policy content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block-level section in the normal flow, not fixed or sticky. It remains at the top of the scrollable content and scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-content-viewer occupies the left column of the main two-column region immediately below the privacy-policy-header. It is presented as a large white card with subtle elevation (shadow or light border) against the light page background. On desktop, this card spans approximately two-thirds of the available content width, corresponding to about 8 of 12 grid columns, and is aligned to the left edge of the centered content container. The card’s top edge lines up horizontally with the top of the privacy-policy-toc card on the right. Internal padding is generous—24–32px on all sides—to mirror the article card in the mockup. The content flows vertically with clear typographic hierarchy: section headings have extra spacing above (16–24px) and 8–12px below; paragraphs maintain comfortable line spacing and modest vertical gaps (8–12px). Any numbered or bulleted lists have single-column layout with 8–12px vertical spacing between items, reinforcing readability. Vertically, the content viewer card is the tallest element in the main region, often extending well past the bottom of the TOC card and driving the majority of page scroll. On tablet and mobile (below roughly 960px), the content viewer becomes full-width and stacks above the TOC card, preserving 24px vertical spacing between them and maintaining similar internal padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level card within the grid. On larger screens it sits in the left column of a two-column layout; on smaller screens it becomes a full-width block stacked above the TOC, all within the normal scrolling flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-toc is positioned to the right of the content viewer within the same horizontal row, forming the secondary column of the two-column layout. On desktop, it spans roughly one-third of the content width (about 4 of 12 grid columns) and aligns its top edge with the top of the privacy-policy-content-viewer card, with a consistent horizontal gap of around 24–32px between the two cards. It appears as a shorter white card with the same visual treatment (border radius, subtle shadow, and 24–32px internal padding) to maintain visual consistency. At the top of the card, a small heading such as ‘On this page’ or ‘Contents’ is left-aligned, with 8–12px spacing before the first TOC item. Below, a vertical list of section links is arranged with compact but readable spacing (8–12px between items), allowing 6–12 links to fit comfortably without making the card feel dense. The card’s height is typically less than the height of the content viewer card; as a result, once the user scrolls past its height, clear whitespace appears beneath it while the main policy text continues. On tablet and mobile breakpoints, the TOC card drops below the content viewer, becomes full-width, and maintains about 24px vertical separation from the card above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the TOC card is positioned in the right column and is sticky within the viewport: after the user scrolls past its initial top position, it remains pinned near the top of the viewport with a consistent top offset (e.g., 24–32px below the global header) until the bottom of the main content area is reached. On smaller screens, the sticky behavior can be disabled or relaxed, with the TOC behaving as a normal block-level section that scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-section-deeplink-handler is an invisible behavioral layer associated with the policy-content-viewer and the TOC. It does not render any visible UI or occupy designated space. Functionally, it synchronizes the URL with the currently active section and enables deep-linking from the TOC or external URLs to specific sections of the policy. Its spatial effect is indirect: it ensures that when a user clicks a TOC link or lands on a URL with a section anchor, the corresponding heading in the left content card scrolls into view, typically positioning that heading a comfortable distance below the global header (e.g., 64–96px) so it is not hidden beneath the dark top bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Has no visual footprint and does not affect layout dimensions. It only influences scroll positions programmatically, offsetting anchor targets so section headings align nicely within the visible area below the fixed global header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-error-state appears in place of the main content region whenever the privacy policy content fails to load. When active, it occupies the same centered max-width container beneath the privacy-policy-header. Instead of rendering the two-column layout, a single, full-width error card is displayed, aligned to the left edge of the content grid and spanning its full width. This card uses the same padding (24–32px) and white background as the other cards to remain visually consistent. Inside, a concise error headline is left-aligned at the top, followed by a short explanatory text and possibly a primary action (e.g., ‘Retry’) arranged in a vertical stack with 8–16px spacing. The card is vertically centered within the available viewport height as much as possible, with at least 40–64px margin above and below, so it feels balanced and clearly distinct from a normal content card. The TOC is not shown when the error state is active, simplifying the layout to a single, focused message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the main two-column content area in the normal document flow. It scrolls normally when content above exceeds the viewport height and does not overlay the site header; it does not behave as a fixed overlay, but rather as an inline, full-width card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-loading-indicator appears while the privacy policy content is being fetched. It is centered within the main content container beneath the privacy-policy-header. In its simplest form, it occupies the space where the left content card would normally sit, displaying a centered spinner or progress indicator with a short status label below it (e.g., ‘Loading privacy policy…’). The indicator is horizontally centered in the content container and positioned with generous vertical spacing—approximately 64–96px below the header—so it sits near the visual center of the viewport on typical laptop screens. Optionally, a faint placeholder block or skeleton card can extend to roughly two-thirds of the content width, matching the future width of the content viewer, to hint at the upcoming layout. The TOC is either hidden or shown as a minimal skeleton to avoid distraction; the focus remains on the loading state in the main area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily occupies the main content region in the normal document flow while data is loading. It does not fix itself to the viewport; instead, it appears below the header and scrolls normally if prior content extends above it. Once loading completes, it is removed from the flow and replaced by the two-column content viewer and TOC.
